title: Probabilistic size-and-shape functional mixed models

reviews.summary:
The paper deals with estimation functional data, i.e. time-series represented as functions – under a specific observation model, that aims to separately account for a fixed effect (modeled as a mean function) and other variations (modeled as noise functions), with the added confounding variable being a norm-preserving warping function, that leads to observed data.

The paper proceeds to develop an Bayesian approach to estimating the model parameters, under certain assumed distributions for the noise terms, and certain basis functions to represent the functions in a finite dimensional space.

Evaluation is shown both on simulated data and some real functional data examples.
reviews.strengths:
The paper does a good job of introducing various temporal warping models, and their intuition on many example data. I found the mathematical and visual explanations of the differences between value preserving and norm preserving warps illuminating. The paper is well positioned to advance the field of functional data analysis as more and more applications get impacted by these methods.

The paper tries to motivate the presented mixed effects models by highlighting historical examples based on 2D shape analysis, which provides good context for the proposed developments. 

The paper also has good strategies for actually implementing the infinite dimensional computations using projections onto basis functions such as B-splines.
reviews.weaknesses:
Comparisons are limited to a publicly available implementation of the technique known as warpMiX. Default parameters are used as given in the R implementation. At the very least some attempt at matching noise variance assumed in the warpmix model and the proposed model should be attempted. Default noise variance parameter in warpmix is at the level of 10^-3 whereas the noise variances for the proposed model are much higher at sigma_c^2 = 0.25, sigma^2 = 1 etc… Are these comparable in any sense, if not how should this comparison be made more fairly? 

Further, the paper cites a wealth of literature that uses a model similar to the one used, at least as far as the assumed norm preserving action model. There are papers by the cited authors that use these models for estimating mean functions from a stack of observed functions, mainly by Kurtek, Srivastava and colleagues. Many of these references are also cited in the paper, but some attempt should be made to compare to these approaches. 

Beyond simulation, for the task of recovery of functions (and functional parameters) under an assumed observation model, evaluation should include some real downstream task. Otherwise, we are only faced with visual assessments of quality. For instance, the results as shown in figure 4 are claimed to be better in the sense that the estimate is ‘as desired and contains sharp features that are representative of the observed data’ (line 351, pg 9). These are at best subjective assessments, and it is unclear if such an estimate is useful in some quantifiable way. There are many ways to take this to the next step of evaluation, to see if some downstream task such as classification shows improvement after the recovery of the needed parameters.
reviews.questions:
I would like to know how well the method could be implemented without using basis function approximations – i.e. representing functions simply by their samples. What gets simplified and what gets complicated. What are the practical implications of assuming a specific family of basis functions such as B-Splines. I did notice the data-driven functional PCA basis was tried as well, in the appendix, which seemed to perform worse than B-splines.

Additional questions are on evaluation, which are described in the experiments section of the review.
reviews.limitations: The paper has a reasonable description of the limitations of the model and its assumptions in section 3.

Summary:
The paper deals with estimation functional data, i.e. time-series represented as functions – under a specific observation model, that aims to separately account for a fixed effect (modeled as a mean function) and other variations (modeled as noise functions), with the added confounding variable being a norm-preserving warping function, that leads to observed data.

The paper proceeds to develop an Bayesian approach to estimating the model parameters, under certain assumed distributions for the noise terms, and certain basis functions to represent the functions in a finite dimensional space.

Evaluation is shown both on simulated data and some real functional data examples.

Strengths:
The paper does a good job of introducing various temporal warping models, and their intuition on many example data. I found the mathematical and visual explanations of the differences between value preserving and norm preserving warps illuminating. The paper is well positioned to advance the field of functional data analysis as more and more applications get impacted by these methods.

The paper tries to motivate the presented mixed effects models by highlighting historical examples based on 2D shape analysis, which provides good context for the proposed developments. 

The paper also has good strategies for actually implementing the infinite dimensional computations using projections onto basis functions such as B-splines.

Weaknesses:
Comparisons are limited to a publicly available implementation of the technique known as warpMiX. Default parameters are used as given in the R implementation. At the very least some attempt at matching noise variance assumed in the warpmix model and the proposed model should be attempted. Default noise variance parameter in warpmix is at the level of 10^-3 whereas the noise variances for the proposed model are much higher at sigma_c^2 = 0.25, sigma^2 = 1 etc… Are these comparable in any sense, if not how should this comparison be made more fairly? 

Further, the paper cites a wealth of literature that uses a model similar to the one used, at least as far as the assumed norm preserving action model. There are papers by the cited authors that use these models for estimating mean functions from a stack of observed functions, mainly by Kurtek, Srivastava and colleagues. Many of these references are also cited in the paper, but some attempt should be made to compare to these approaches. 

Beyond simulation, for the task of recovery of functions (and functional parameters) under an assumed observation model, evaluation should include some real downstream task. Otherwise, we are only faced with visual assessments of quality. For instance, the results as shown in figure 4 are claimed to be better in the sense that the estimate is ‘as desired and contains sharp features that are representative of the observed data’ (line 351, pg 9). These are at best subjective assessments, and it is unclear if such an estimate is useful in some quantifiable way. There are many ways to take this to the next step of evaluation, to see if some downstream task such as classification shows improvement after the recovery of the needed parameters.

Questions:
I would like to know how well the method could be implemented without using basis function approximations – i.e. representing functions simply by their samples. What gets simplified and what gets complicated. What are the practical implications of assuming a specific family of basis functions such as B-Splines. I did notice the data-driven functional PCA basis was tried as well, in the appendix, which seemed to perform worse than B-splines.

Additional questions are on evaluation, which are described in the experiments section of the review.

Limitations:
The paper has a reasonable description of the limitations of the model and its assumptions in section 3.

Rating:
7
reviews.text:
# Hybrid Peer Review

## Summary

This paper proposes a Bayesian functional mixed model for inferring the size-and-shape of a fixed effect function μ in functional data exhibiting phase and amplitude variability. The key innovation is employing the norm-preserving action of phase functions (characterized by unitary transformations on the L² Hilbert space) rather than the traditional value-preserving action used in prior work. The authors develop a complete Bayesian framework with two flexible prior models for phase functions (PM1: one-parameter family; PM2: nonparametric Dirichlet-process-based), implement MCMC inference using basis function approximations, and demonstrate performance on simulated and real data examples including Berkeley growth curves and ECG complexes. The approach is compared primarily to the state-of-the-art frequentist method warpMix, showing competitive or superior performance in several settings.

## Strengths

The paper makes several solid contributions to functional data analysis. First, the mathematical and visual explanations of the distinction between value-preserving and norm-preserving warping functions are illuminating and well-presented. The connection to landmark-based shape analysis provides good intuition for why the norm-preserving perspective is geometrically motivated. The group-theoretic treatment of phase functions is rigorous, and the paper carefully develops the Lie group structure with appropriate mathematical detail.

Second, the paper provides a comprehensive Bayesian framework with complete specifications for priors, likelihoods, and MCMC algorithms. The development of two flexible prior models for phase functions—one parsimonious and one nonparametric—demonstrates thoughtful modeling choices. The practical implementation strategies for finite-dimensional approximations using B-splines and other basis functions are well-developed, addressing the challenge of representing infinite-dimensional computations in a tractable manner.

Third, the experimental validation is extensive, including multiple simulations, real data applications, sensitivity analyses (Appendix H), and systematic comparisons to state-of-the-art methods. The authors provide code and detailed instructions for reproducibility, and MCMC diagnostics (Appendix F) indicate satisfactory convergence. The paper is well-positioned to advance the field of functional data analysis as applications increasingly encounter phase variability in temporal data.

## Major Weaknesses

Despite these strengths, the paper has several significant limitations that warrant careful consideration.

**Lack of Theoretical Justification.** The most critical weakness is the acknowledged absence of theoretical support for the proposed approach. The authors explicitly state in Section 5: "What is lacking is theoretical support for the same, and this is work in progress." The paper provides no identifiability results, convergence guarantees, or formal conditions under which the size-and-shape of μ can be reliably recovered. This is a substantial gap for a methodological paper. The central claim—that the larger equivalence class under norm-preserving action (point iii, Section 2) facilitates reliable recovery of μ—is stated without rigorous justification. While intuitively appealing and empirically supported, this claim lacks formal proof or even formal conditions specifying when reliable recovery is possible. Under what conditions do violations of model assumptions lead to failure? What are the posterior contraction rates? These fundamental questions remain unanswered.

**Computational Efficiency.** The computational cost is prohibitive for practical applications. For n=30 functions at T=50 discretization points, MCMC inference requires approximately 93-111 minutes (300,000 iterations). The authors acknowledge in Section 5 that "we do not make any claims on efficiency of the computational algorithms," but offer no concrete solutions or timeline for improvement. Scalability to realistic datasets with n>100 and T>100 is unclear and likely infeasible. While the authors mention that variational inference or other approximations could be explored, no such work is presented. This computational burden severely limits the practical applicability of the method.

**Limited and Potentially Unfair Comparisons.** The primary comparison is to the frequentist method warpMix using default parameters. However, the noise variance assumptions differ substantially between the methods: warpMix's default noise variance is at the level of 10⁻³, whereas the proposed model uses much higher values (σ_c² = 0.25, σ² = 1, etc.). It is unclear whether these are comparable, and the paper makes no attempt to match these parameters for a fair comparison. Additionally, comparison to other Bayesian functional mixed models is limited; the comparison to Cheng et al. (2016) is relegated to Appendix A. More critically, in Example 2, data are generated from the warpMix model (which uses value-preserving actions) but fit with the proposed norm-preserving model. While the proposed method performs competitively, this somewhat undermines the claim that norm-preserving action is necessary for reliable recovery—if the method works well on value-preserving data, when is the distinction practically important?

Furthermore, the paper cites a substantial body of work by Kurtek, Srivastava, and colleagues on norm-preserving models for functional data and shape analysis, yet makes limited attempt to compare to these existing approaches or clearly articulate what methodological advances beyond prior work are provided. The comparison scope is narrow, limited primarily to warpMix and one Bayesian baseline, whereas comparison to other functional mixed model approaches (e.g., those by Morris & Carroll, Guo, Chen & Wang) would better contextualize the performance gains.

**Hyperparameter and Basis Selection.** The number of basis functions (Bf and Br) must be specified a priori, and while Appendix H shows some robustness to over-specification, underspecification clearly degrades performance (Figure 10). The paper provides limited principled guidance for practitioners on how to choose these parameters for new applications. The choice of basis functions (modified Fourier for μ, B-splines for vi) appears somewhat arbitrary. Although the authors argue that the norm-preserving action provides flexibility such that basis choice is "not crucial" (Section 5), Table 1 shows non-negligible differences between models using different bases (e.g., Model 1-F vs. Model 1-B, Model 2-F vs. Model 2-B). This apparent contradiction suggests that basis selection does matter in practice, yet systematic guidance is absent. The restriction of PM1 to γ(t) = t + αᵢt(1-t) also limits expressiveness by excluding inflection points, though PM2 addresses this at computational cost.

## Minor Weaknesses and Clarifications Needed

**Evaluation Beyond Visual Assessment.** The paper relies heavily on visual assessments of quality. For instance, the results in Figure 4 are claimed to be "as desired and contains sharp features that are representative of the observed data" (line 351), but these are subjective assessments. For real data examples where ground truth is unavailable, it is unclear whether the recovered estimates are useful in any quantifiable way. The paper would be substantially strengthened by downstream task evaluation—for example, demonstrating that classification accuracy improves after parameter recovery, or that downstream inference on recovered parameters has meaningful practical consequences. Without such validation, the practical utility of the method remains undemonstrated.

**Model Assumptions and Robustness.** The paper assumes common discretization across observations, independent Gaussian errors, and specific basis function families. While the authors note these can be relaxed "at some computational cost," the practical implications of violations are not thoroughly explored. Example 2 partially addresses robustness when data are generated under a different model (value-preserving action), but more systematic investigation of sensitivity to model misspecification—including non-Gaussian errors, dependent error structures, and irregular sampling—would strengthen claims. The assumption that ¯γ ∈ Γ due to convexity of Γ (an infinite-dimensional space) also requires more rigorous justification.

**Basis Function Representation Without Basis Approximations.** The paper does not discuss the practical and theoretical implications of the basis function approximation itself. How would the method be implemented using only sampled function values without basis representations? What computational and inferential challenges would arise? The paper mentions that data-driven functional PCA basis was tried in the appendix and performed worse than B-splines, but does not explain why or explore whether this limitation could be addressed.

**Prior Sensitivity.** While Appendix H studies hyperparameter misspecification, sensitivity to prior choices is not explored. The prior variances (10000IBf) and hyperparameter choices (e.g., θγ = 30 in PM2) are weakly informative but their impact on posterior inference is unclear. Was θγ tuned on the data? How sensitive are results to this choice?

**Identifiability Between Phase and Amplitude Variation.** The relationship between phase functions γᵢ and random amplitude effects vᵢ is not fully explored. Can the model reliably distinguish between these sources of variation, or are there identifiability issues? This is crucial for the interpretation of recovered parameters but is not addressed.

## Questions for Authors

1. **Theoretical foundations:** Can you provide formal conditions—even partial results—under which the size-and-

reviews.summary: The paper studies the problem of recovering a fixed effect function µ in functional mixed models, where measurement errors and object-level phase variations make the task difficult. It focuses on disentangling the size-and-shape characteristics of µ, which remain invariant under certain transformations. The authors hypothesize that it is feasible to reliably recover the size-and-shape of µ using a Bayesian functional mixed model framework. The formulation is justified by numerical experiments on synthetic and real data.
reviews.strengths:
1. The presentation over all is clear, self-contained, and precise. While this might sound standard in the past, I find these properties rare in papers these days, so I appreciate them even more. 
2. The formulation is carefully backed up by justifications for the choice of components (e.g., basis functions, prior distributions) as well as numerical experiments.
reviews.weaknesses:
## Significance:



1. The main proposal of this paper is to integrate phase variations into the model in a phase-preserving manner (equations 3 and 4). Nonetheless, I am struggling to understand what is the significance of such a model. More precisely,
    1. Is this a reasonable model for real applications? Namely, are there some practical applications in which we have reasons to believe the phase functions act on the fixed effect mu in a phase-preserving way?
    2. What are the main conceptual benefits and drawbacks of phase-preserving compared to value-preserving actions? The only answer I seem to find is in lines 142-147, but I do not understand the statement there as I detail in “Clarity”.
2. The comparison of the proposed method with warpMix on real data (Figure 4) seems limited, and this comment relate to the one above on real applications. It is hard to say which method is better when there is no ground-truth - the two methods give different estimates of mu, but it is hard to say which one makes more sense. 

## Clarity:



1. Lines 142-147: Why is “The equivalence class of functions having the same size-and-shape under the norm-preserving action is in an appropriate sense ‘larger’ than the one under the value-preserving action”? 
    1. The explanation following the colon is not sufficient, since the same could be said for value-preserving action: for any g and gamma(t), one can find f(t) := g(gamma^{-1}(t)) such that f(gamma(t)) = g(gamma^{-1}(gamma(t))) = g(t).
    2. The explanation in footnote 1 is a bit hand-wavy and it would be great if rigorous proof could be provided or referred to.

## Novelty:


The idea that the parameterization of the model is not minimal and therefore there is the ambiguity of having an equivalence class of solutions has been there for a long time, as the authors also point out in equation (2). Since (2) is an example in geometric vision which i am a bit more familiar with, I would like to point out more high-level connections here. 


1. A basic idea to resolve the ambiguity is to enforce constraints that favors certain elements in each equivalence class than others. This is more or less what the authors are doing near line 76. Note however that: while two sets of parameters can be equivalent in the sense that if there were no noise then the two sets of parameters explain the observation equally well, in the noisy case the two sets of parameters could handle noise very differently. Therefore, one needs to be smart on which elements in each equivalence class to pick. For example, the work of [A] argues that picking a particular transformation (normalization) in the parameters improves numerical stability of the estimation in the noisy case.
2. Following the same line, two sets of “equivalent” parameters could also be favored differently when the data are corrupted by outliers. An alternative idea to resolve the ambiguity is to estimate the entire equivalent class (rather than finding an element of it), which is for example the path explored in the work of [B].

So far i do not see a strong need for the current paper to exhaust all options, but it would be great if the authors could discuss on how optimal the choice of line 76 is, what might be potential alternatives, etc.

[A] R. I. Hartley, “In defense of the eight-point algorithm,” IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence, 1997.

[B] Ding, T., Yang, Y., Zhu, Z., Robinson, D. P., Vidal, R., Kneip, L., & Tsakiris, M. C, “Robust homography estimation via dual principal component pursuit”, In the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition, 2020.
reviews.questions:
## Minor comments / questions:



1. Could you comment on how does using MCMC to get parameters from the posterior compare to using optimization, e.g., by writing down the log likelihood and then maximize it over the parameters?
2. The introduction was somewhat confusing, as illustrated below. 
    3. The first paragraph motivates the reader with the Berkeley growth data example, saying that a problem for modeling is that the sample size is smaller than the dimension observation, so one must assume prior / constraints in the hypothesis class. Yet the model in equation (1) is rather general and does not assume much prior for recovery.
    4. Now the second paragraph makes the model even more complicated and harder to estimate by introducing phase variability. In particular, why isn’t model (1) sufficient and why does phase variability make sense? 

    To sum up, I think there are multiple problems motivated and highlighted along the way which somehow buries the key issue of phase variability. 

3. The reviewer is grateful that some basics on phase functions and size-and-shape-preserving transformations are provided in section 2. Nonetheless, when I read Section 2 (before going through the rest of the paper) it is unclear why are these properties important - are they going to contribute to the solution? Which key properties allow you to do something that previously was not done? This for example can be said in the preamble of section 2 to make the flow smoother.
4. Line 119: gamma in Gamma?
reviews.limitations: See above

1. Could you comment on how does using MCMC to get parameters from the posterior compare to using optimization, e.g., by writing down the log likelihood and then maximize it over the parameters?
2. The introduction was somewhat confusing, as illustrated below. 
    3. The first paragraph motivates the reader with the Berkeley growth data example, saying that a problem for modeling is that the sample size is smaller than the dimension observation, so one must assume prior / constraints in the hypothesis class. Yet the model in equation (1) is rather general and does not assume much prior for recovery.
    4. Now the second paragraph makes the model even more complicated and harder to estimate by introducing phase variability. In particular, why isn’t model (1) sufficient and why does phase variability make sense?

Nonetheless, when I read Section 2 (before going through the rest of the paper) it is unclear why are these properties important - are they going to contribute to the solution? Which key properties allow you to do something that previously was not done? This for example can be said in the preamble of section 2 to make the flow smoother.

reviews.text:
# Hybrid Peer Review

## Summary

This paper proposes a Bayesian functional mixed model for inferring the size-and-shape of a fixed effect function µ in functional data with phase and amplitude variability. The key innovation is employing the norm-preserving action of phase functions (diffeomorphisms characterized by unitary transformations on L²) rather than the traditional value-preserving action. The authors decompose functional observations as fi = Dγi(µ + vi + ϵi), where Dγi represents a size-and-shape-preserving rotation, vi captures amplitude deviations, and ϵi is measurement error. The framework includes two flexible prior models for phase functions (PM1: one-parameter family; PM2: Dirichlet process-based nonparametric model) and employs MCMC for inference. The approach is demonstrated on simulated and real data (Berkeley growth curves, ECG complexes), showing superior performance compared to state-of-the-art methods like warpMix.

## Strengths

1. **Clear and rigorous presentation**: The paper is well-written, self-contained, and mathematically precise. The careful development of the Lie group structure of phase functions and the connection to unitary transformations in Hilbert space provides elegant theoretical grounding. The distinction between value-preserving and norm-preserving actions is clearly articulated with good visual illustrations (Figure 1).

2. **Novel geometric perspective**: The use of norm-preserving actions to define size-and-shape as a recoverable geometric property is creative and well-motivated. The connection to landmark-based shape analysis provides good intuition for why this perspective might be valuable. The mathematical framework leverages group theory and differential geometry appropriately.

3. **Comprehensive framework with thorough justification**: The formulation is carefully backed up by justifications for the choice of components (basis functions, prior distributions). Two flexible prior models for phase functions are proposed, with detailed MCMC algorithms provided. The use of group structure in proposal distributions is clever, and the paper provides complete specifications with explicit acceptance ratios and thorough derivations in appendices.

4. **Extensive experimental validation**: The paper includes extensive simulations (Examples 1-2), real data applications (Berkeley, PQRST), sensitivity analyses (Appendix H), and systematic comparisons with state-of-the-art methods. MCMC diagnostics appear satisfactory, and reproducibility is enhanced through provided code and data.

5. **Honest acknowledgment of limitations**: The authors explicitly acknowledge the lack of theoretical guarantees, computational costs, and modeling assumptions, demonstrating intellectual honesty about the work's scope.

## Major Weaknesses

### 1. Lack of Theoretical Foundation

The paper explicitly states (Section 5) that "what is lacking is theoretical support for the same, and this is work in progress." This is a significant gap. The authors provide no identifiability results, convergence guarantees, or formal conditions under which size-and-shape can be reliably recovered. The central claim that "it is possible to recover the size-and-shape of µ" relies entirely on empirical validation, which is insufficient for a methodological contribution.

Critically, the claim that equivalence classes under norm-preserving action are "in an appropriate sense 'larger'" than under value-preserving action (lines 142-147) lacks rigorous justification. The explanation provided is insufficient: as noted, for any g and γ(t), one can find f(t) := g(γ^{-1}(t)) such that f(γ(t)) = g(t), which applies equally to value-preserving actions. While footnote 1 attempts to address this through a reference to Bogachev (1998), the argument remains hand-wavy. A formal proof or rigorous empirical demonstration specific to functional data would be needed to support this critical motivation. Under what conditions does the larger equivalence class actually lead to improved recovery? This needs theoretical support, not just intuition.

### 2. Significance and Motivation for the Model

The significance of the proposed norm-preserving model remains unclear:

(a) **Applicability to real problems**: Are there compelling practical applications where phase functions demonstrably act on µ in a norm-preserving manner? The paper motivates the problem using Berkeley growth data and ECG complexes, but it is not evident that these applications truly exhibit norm-preserving phase variation rather than value-preserving variation. Example 2 (Section 4.1) is instructive here: data generated from the warpMix model (value-preserving action) are fit with the proposed norm-preserving method, and the method still performs competitively. While presented as demonstrating robustness, this somewhat undermines the necessity of the norm-preserving framework.

(b) **Conceptual benefits and drawbacks**: Beyond the size-and-shape equivalence class argument (which lacks rigor), what are the fundamental conceptual advantages of norm-preserving over value-preserving actions? The paper does not clearly articulate when practitioners should prefer one over the other. The practical significance of recovering size-and-shape versus classical shape remains abstract and unvalidated by domain expertise.

(c) **Limited real data validation**: The comparison with warpMix on real data (Figure 4) is problematic. When there is no ground truth, it is difficult to say which method produces more sensible estimates. The two methods yield different estimates of µ, but without validation by domain experts or external benchmarks, it is unclear which is preferable. For the Berkeley growth data and PQRST examples, can the recovered size-and-shape estimates be validated against domain knowledge?

### 3. Computational Efficiency and Scalability

The paper explicitly states (Section 5): "We do not make any claims on efficiency of the computational algorithms." For n=30 functions at T=50 discretization points, MCMC requires ~93-111 minutes for 300,000 iterations. This is prohibitive for practical applications. Real datasets often have n > 100 and T > 100, but the paper provides no analysis of how the method scales or concrete solutions for improvement.

While the authors acknowledge this limitation, they offer no principled alternatives such as variational inference, expectation-propagation, or gradient-based approximations. The statement "there is room for improvement" is vague and does not address the fundamental scalability concern. This severely limits the practical applicability of the method and raises questions about whether the empirical gains over faster alternatives (like warpMix) justify the computational cost.

### 4. Theoretical Novelty and Connection to Existing Work

The idea that model parameterization introduces ambiguity and equivalence classes of solutions is well-established (as the authors note with equation 2). The authors appropriately cite geometric vision literature, but the novelty of their contribution relative to existing approaches for resolving this ambiguity is not clearly articulated.

Two important points from the literature deserve more discussion:

(a) **Constraint selection under noise**: While enforcing constraints to favor certain elements in equivalence classes (near line 76) is standard, the choice of which constraints to use is non-trivial in the noisy case. Two sets of parameters equivalent in the noise-free case may handle noise very differently. The literature on normalization and numerical stability (e.g., Hartley, 1997) suggests that the choice of constraint significantly impacts estimation robustness. The paper does not discuss whether the chosen centering procedure (orbit centering via ¯γ) is optimal or how it compares to alternatives.

(b) **Alternative approaches**: Rather than selecting a single element from each equivalence class, one could estimate the entire equivalence class. Recent work (e.g., Ding et al., 2020) explores such approaches. The paper would benefit from discussing why focusing on a single representative is preferable and what is lost by not estimating the full class.

## Minor Weaknesses and Clarity Issues

### 1. Introduction and Motivation Flow

The introduction is somewhat confusing:

- The first paragraph motivates the problem using Berkeley growth data, arguing that small sample size relative to dimension necessitates priors or constraints. Yet equation (1) presents a rather general model without strong priors.
- The second paragraph then introduces phase variability, further complicating the model. The logical flow from motivation to proposed solution is unclear: Why isn't model (1) sufficient? Why does phase variability specifically make sense for these applications?

This buries the key issue of phase variability under multiple layers of motivation. The paper would benefit from a clearer problem statement upfront.

### 2. Section 2 Context

Section 2 provides useful background on phase functions and size-and-shape-preserving transformations, but the preamble does not explain why these properties are important or how they will enable new capabilities. It is unclear which key properties allow the authors to solve problems previously unsolved. Adding a brief statement of what properties will be exploited would improve readability.

### 3. Hyperparameter Selection and Basis Flexibility

While the norm-preserving action provides flexibility through rotation of the basis, the choice of basis functions (modified Fourier for µ, B-splines for
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
The paper studies the problem of recovering a fixed effect function µ in functional mixed models, where measurement errors and object-level phase variations make the task difficult. It focuses on disentangling the size-and-shape characteristics of µ, which remain invariant under certain transformations. The authors hypothesize that it is feasible to reliably recover the size-and-shape of µ using a Bayesian functional mixed model framework. The formulation is justified by numerical experiments on synthetic and real data.

Strengths:
1. The presentation over all is clear, self-contained, and precise. While this might sound standard in the past, I find these properties rare in papers these days, so I appreciate them even more. 
2. The formulation is carefully backed up by justifications for the choice of components (e.g., basis functions, prior distributions) as well as numerical experiments.

Weaknesses:
## Significance:



1. The main proposal of this paper is to integrate phase variations into the model in a phase-preserving manner (equations 3 and 4). Nonetheless, I am struggling to understand what is the significance of such a model. More precisely,
    1. Is this a reasonable model for real applications? Namely, are there some practical applications in which we have reasons to believe the phase functions act on the fixed effect mu in a phase-preserving way?
    2. What are the main conceptual benefits and drawbacks of phase-preserving compared to value-preserving actions? The only answer I seem to find is in lines 142-147, but I do not understand the statement there as I detail in “Clarity”.
2. The comparison of the proposed method with warpMix on real data (Figure 4) seems limited, and this comment relate to the one above on real applications. It is hard to say which method is better when there is no ground-truth - the two methods give different estimates of mu, but it is hard to say which one makes more sense. 

## Clarity:



1. Lines 142-147: Why is “The equivalence class of functions having the same size-and-shape under the norm-preserving action is in an appropriate sense ‘larger’ than the one under the value-preserving action”? 
    1. The explanation following the colon is not sufficient, since the same could be said for value-preserving action: for any g and gamma(t), one can find f(t) := g(gamma^{-1}(t)) such that f(gamma(t)) = g(gamma^{-1}(gamma(t))) = g(t).
    2. The explanation in footnote 1 is a bit hand-wavy and it would be great if rigorous proof could be provided or referred to.

## Novelty:


The idea that the parameterization of the model is not minimal and therefore there is the ambiguity of having an equivalence class of solutions has been there for a long time, as the authors also point out in equation (2). Since (2) is an example in geometric vision which i am a bit more familiar with, I would like to point out more high-level connections here. 


1. A basic idea to resolve the ambiguity is to enforce constraints that favors certain elements in each equivalence class than others. This is more or less what the authors are doing near line 76. Note however that: while two sets of parameters can be equivalent in the sense that if there were no noise then the two sets of parameters explain the observation equally well, in the noisy case the two sets of parameters could handle noise very differently. Therefore, one needs to be smart on which elements in each equivalence class to pick. For example, the work of [A] argues that picking a particular transformation (normalization) in the parameters improves numerical stability of the estimation in the noisy case.
2. Following the same line, two sets of “equivalent” parameters could also be favored differently when the data are corrupted by outliers. An alternative idea to resolve the ambiguity is to estimate the entire equivalent class (rather than finding an element of it), which is for example the path explored in the work of [B].

So far i do not see a strong need for the current paper to exhaust all options, but it would be great if the authors could discuss on how optimal the choice of line 76 is, what might be potential alternatives, etc.

[A] R. I. Hartley, “In defense of the eight-point algorithm,” IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence, 1997.

[B] Ding, T., Yang, Y., Zhu, Z., Robinson, D. P., Vidal, R., Kneip, L., & Tsakiris, M. C, “Robust homography estimation via dual principal component pursuit”, In the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition, 2020.

Questions:
## Minor comments / questions:



1. Could you comment on how does using MCMC to get parameters from the posterior compare to using optimization, e.g., by writing down the log likelihood and then maximize it over the parameters?
2. The introduction was somewhat confusing, as illustrated below. 
    3. The first paragraph motivates the reader with the Berkeley growth data example, saying that a problem for modeling is that the sample size is smaller than the dimension observation, so one must assume prior / constraints in the hypothesis class. Yet the model in equation (1) is rather general and does not assume much prior for recovery.
    4. Now the second paragraph makes the model even more complicated and harder to estimate by introducing phase variability. In particular, why isn’t model (1) sufficient and why does phase variability make sense? 

    To sum up, I think there are multiple problems motivated and highlighted along the way which somehow buries the key issue of phase variability. 

3. The reviewer is grateful that some basics on phase functions and size-and-shape-preserving transformations are provided in section 2. Nonetheless, when I read Section 2 (before going through the rest of the paper) it is unclear why are these properties important - are they going to contribute to the solution? Which key properties allow you to do something that previously was not done? This for example can be said in the preamble of section 2 to make the flow smoother.
4. Line 119: gamma in Gamma?

Limitations:
See above

Rating:
7

Summary:
The paper considers uncertainty quantification for one-dimensional regression tasks, where the observations are noisy. A rather advanced additive model considering invariances under space-time unitary transformations is proposted. Numerical experiments demonstrate the superiority of the approach over other state-of-the-art methods.

Strengths:
- Due to the strong modeling assumptions, on synthethic datasets, the method can recover the true signal even in situations where there is lots of noise and classical methods (such as GPs) may fail. 
- The methods seems to be useful to analyze the Berkley growth spurt data set.

Weaknesses:
- It is unclear whether the work will be of interest to the wider machine learning audience and the considered topic and datasets feel more of a niche. 
- Despite the well-written introduction, the paper was quite difficult to understand due to the advanced math and notations -- after reading it I am still quite unsure why I should really care about functional mixed models and their Bayesian inference.

Questions:
I am slightly puzzled as of why NeurIPS was chosen as a venue for this paper - the work may not get the appreciation here it deserves. I spent quite some time on trying to understand the paper, but its motivations and applications to wider machine learning remain unclear. Perhaps the paper may be better suited towards a statistics journal, such as the ones referenced often in the paper?   If more general motivations are clarified, I am considering to change my score, but accessibility issues are remaining.

All limitations are adequately addressed.

# Hybrid Peer Review

## Summary

This paper proposes a Bayesian functional mixed model for uncertainty quantification in one-dimensional regression with noisy observations. The key innovation is employing the norm-preserving action of phase functions (characterized by unitary transformations on the L² Hilbert space) rather than the traditional value-preserving action to recover the size-and-shape of a fixed effect function µ. The authors develop a comprehensive Bayesian framework with two flexible prior models for phase functions (a one-parameter family and a nonparametric Dirichlet-process-based model), implement MCMC inference with detailed algorithmic specifications, and demonstrate superior performance compared to state-of-the-art methods like warpMix on simulated and real datasets including Berkeley growth curves and ECG complexes.

## Strengths

**Novel theoretical perspective and mathematical rigor**: The use of norm-preserving actions to define size-and-shape as a recoverable geometric property is creative and well-motivated. The connection to statistical shape analysis of landmark data provides good intuition for the approach. The paper carefully develops the Lie group structure of phase functions and their actions on L², with the distinction between value-preserving and norm-preserving actions clearly articulated through visual illustrations and mathematical exposition. The group-theoretic treatment is sophisticated and the connection to unitary transformations in Hilbert space is elegant.

**Strong empirical performance on synthetic data**: Due to the strong modeling assumptions, the method can recover the true signal even in situations with substantial noise where classical methods (such as GPs) fail. The extensive simulations in Examples 1-2 demonstrate this advantage convincingly, with quantitative metrics showing clear improvements over warpMix in many settings.

**Comprehensive experimental validation and reproducibility**: The paper includes extensive simulations, real data applications (Berkeley growth curves, PQRST ECG data), and additional datasets in appendices. Comparisons to state-of-the-art methods are systematic, with thorough derivations provided in appendices and MCMC diagnostics (Appendix F) showing good convergence. Code, data, and detailed hyperparameter specifications are supplied, enhancing reproducibility.

**Practical utility on real datasets**: The method appears useful for analyzing the Berkeley growth spurt dataset and ECG complexes, demonstrating practical applicability beyond toy examples. The framework accommodates unrestricted nonparametric phase functions without restrictive rank assumptions on error covariance, addressing real limitations of prior work.

**Honest about limitations**: The authors openly acknowledge computational costs, lack of theoretical guarantees, and modeling assumptions, which is commendable for a methodological paper.

## Major Weaknesses

**Lack of theoretical justification and identifiability analysis**: The paper explicitly acknowledges (Section 5) that "what is lacking is theoretical support" for reliable recovery of size-and-shape. This is a significant gap. No identifiability conditions, convergence rates, or formal guarantees on what properties of µ can be reliably recovered are provided. The claim that size-and-shape can be reliably recovered is purely empirical. Critically, the paper lacks formal justification for a central motivation—the claim (point iii, Section 2) that equivalence classes under norm-preserving action have larger measure, which is stated without proof or empirical validation despite being central to the argument. It remains unclear under what conditions the size-and-shape of µ is identifiable and under what conditions the method distinguishes between phase variation (γᵢ) and amplitude variation (vᵢ), or whether these sources of variation could be confounded.

**Accessibility and scope concerns**: Despite the well-written introduction, the paper is quite difficult to understand due to advanced mathematics and dense notation. The heavy use of operator notation (Dγ, composition, group operations) may limit accessibility to the broader machine learning audience. After careful reading, the fundamental motivation remains unclear—it is not obvious why practitioners should care about functional mixed models and their Bayesian inference, or when size-and-shape recovery is preferable to traditional shape recovery in practice. The practical significance of recovering size-and-shape versus classical shape is not clearly articulated. Moreover, it is unclear whether this work will be of interest to the wider machine learning audience; the considered topic and datasets feel more specialized, raising questions about venue appropriateness. The paper may be better suited to a statistics journal given its focus and the statistical nature of the contributions.

**Computational efficiency is prohibitive**: MCMC requires 93-111 minutes for just 300,000 iterations on n=30 functions at T=50 discretization points. The authors explicitly state (Section 5) "We do not make any claims on efficiency of the computational algorithms." This is a substantial limitation that severely restricts practical applicability. Real datasets often have n>100 and T>100, but the paper provides no analysis of scalability or concrete solutions. While the authors acknowledge "there is room for improvement," no concrete algorithmic improvements (e.g., variational inference, expectation-propagation, gradient-based methods, or parallelization strategies) are explored or proposed. The scalability of the method to realistic problem sizes remains fundamentally undemonstrated.

## Minor Weaknesses and Questions

**Hyperparameter and basis selection**: The number of basis functions (Bf, Br) must be specified a priori, and while Appendix H shows some robustness to over-specification, underspecification of Bf clearly degrades performance substantially (Figure 10). No principled selection procedure is provided for practitioners. The choice of basis functions (modified Fourier for µ, B-splines for vᵢ) appears somewhat arbitrary. While the authors argue that the norm-preserving action provides flexibility through basis rotation, the claim that basis choice is "not crucial" (Section 5) contradicts observed sensitivity in Table 1 (Model 1-F vs Model 1-B differences are non-negligible). Additionally, Appendix G shows poor performance with FPCA basis, suggesting this limitation is more fundamental than claimed. Sensitivity to prior hyperparameter choices (e.g., θγ = 30 in PM2, prior variance 10000IBf) is not thoroughly explored.

**Limited scope of phase variation in PM1**: The one-parameter family (PM1) restricts phase functions to γ(t) = t + αᵢt(1-t), excluding inflection points and limiting expressiveness. While PM2 addresses this with nonparametric modeling, computational cost increases substantially, and the scalability of PM2 is not thoroughly explored.

**Comparison limitations and fairness**: The main comparison is to warpMix (frequentist); comparison to the Bayesian method (Cheng et al., 2016) is relegated to Appendix A, limiting context. More extensive comparisons with other functional mixed model approaches would strengthen claims. In Example 2, data are deliberately generated from the warpMix model (value-preserving action), yet the proposed norm-preserving method still performs competitively. While this is presented as evidence of robustness (point iii, Section 2), it somewhat undermines the claim that norm-preserving action is necessary for reliable recovery. A fairer comparison would test fitting warpMix-generated data with a model using value-preserving actions to isolate the effect of the action choice. Additionally, in the PQRST example, warpMix failed to produce estimates—it is unclear whether this reflects a fundamental limitation or an implementation issue.

**Model assumptions and robustness**: The model assumes common discretization, independent Gaussian errors, and specific basis function forms. While the authors note these can be relaxed "at computational cost," the practical implications of violations are not explored empirically. Robustness to non-Gaussian errors, autocorrelated measurement error, or irregular discretization is not investigated. Example 2 provides one case of robustness to model misspecification, but more systematic analysis would strengthen claims. Sensitivity to the assumption that ¯γ ∈ Γ due to convexity also warrants scrutiny, as the space Γ is infinite-dimensional.

**Limited real data validation**: For real data examples (Berkeley, PQRST), there is no ground truth µ. How can we validate that the recovered size-and-shape is "correct"? Domain-expert assessment or external validation would strengthen claims. All real data examples are relatively small (n ≤ 93) and low-dimensional (T ≤ 50), limiting generalizability claims.

**Inconsistent empirical advantages**: In Table 1, some comparisons show only modest improvements (e.g., Model 2-B achieves 8.2% improvement over warpMix for µ₁). It is unclear how practitioners should determine whether such improvements are meaningful for their applications or what minimum sample size is required for the method to show clear advantages.

## Questions for Authors

1. Can you provide theoretical justification or at least formal conditions under which the size-and-shape of µ is identifiable and recoverable? What properties of the model (basis dimension, prior specifications, sample size) ensure reliable inference?

2. The claim that equivalence classes under norm-preserving action have larger measure (point

reviews.text:
# Hybrid Peer Review

## Summary

This paper proposes a Bayesian functional mixed model for inferring the size-and-shape of a fixed effect function μ in functional data with phase and amplitude variability. The key innovation is employing the norm-preserving action of phase functions (characterized by unitary transformations on the Hilbert space L²) rather than the traditional value-preserving action used in prior work. This geometric perspective allows the authors to decompose functional observations as f_i = D_γ_i(μ + v_i + ε_i), where D_γ_i represents a size-and-shape-preserving rotation, v_i captures amplitude deviations, and ε_i is measurement error. The authors develop a comprehensive Bayesian framework with two flexible prior models for phase functions (PM1: a one-parameter family; PM2: a nonparametric Dirichlet process-based model), implement MCMC inference with detailed algorithmic specifications, and demonstrate superior empirical performance compared to the state-of-the-art frequentist method warpMix on both simulated and real datasets.

## Strengths

1. **Novel and well-motivated geometric perspective**: The use of norm-preserving actions to define size-and-shape as a recoverable geometric property is creative and mathematically elegant. The distinction between value-preserving and norm-preserving actions is clearly articulated with good visual illustrations, and the connection to statistical shape analysis of landmark data provides valuable intuition for the approach. The claim that equivalence classes under norm-preserving actions have larger measure is intuitively appealing and well-presented, even if not rigorously proven in this context.

2. **Rigorous mathematical framework**: The paper carefully develops the functional mixed model theory, leveraging group theory and differential geometry appropriately. The treatment of the Lie group structure of phase functions and its connection to unitary transformations in Hilbert space is sophisticated and provides solid theoretical grounding. Detailed derivations of the Metropolis-Hastings acceptance ratios and MCMC algorithms are provided in comprehensive appendices, facilitating reproducibility and understanding.

3. **Comprehensive Bayesian framework**: The paper provides complete specifications for two prior models on phase functions, detailed likelihood formulations, and explicit MCMC algorithms. The flexibility to accommodate unrestricted nonparametric phase functions without restrictive rank assumptions on the error covariance process represents a practical advance over prior work.

4. **Extensive experimental validation**: The paper includes multiple simulation studies (Examples 1-2), real data applications (Berkeley growth curves, ECG complexes), sensitivity analyses (Appendix H), and systematic comparisons with state-of-the-art methods. The empirical results demonstrate consistent improvements over warpMix on several benchmarks, particularly for complex functions. The authors also provide evidence that the method performs competitively even when data are generated from the value-preserving action model (Example 2), suggesting robustness to model misspecification.

5. **Reproducibility and transparency**: Code and data are provided with clear instructions. MCMC diagnostics appear satisfactory (Appendix F), and the authors are honest about limitations and areas for future work. The sensitivity analyses in Appendix H provide some reassurance regarding hyperparameter robustness.

## Major Weaknesses

1. **Lack of theoretical justification and guarantees**: The authors explicitly acknowledge (Section 5) that "what is lacking is theoretical support for the same, and this is work in progress." This is a significant limitation for a methodological paper. The work provides no identifiability conditions, convergence rate analysis, or formal guarantees regarding when and why size-and-shape can be reliably recovered. While the empirical validation is encouraging, the claim that size-and-shape is reliably recoverable remains purely empirical. Specifically, the paper lacks:
   - Formal conditions on sample size n, discretization points T, basis dimensions B_f and B_r, and prior specifications that ensure reliable inference
   - Analysis of what properties of μ can be reliably recovered and under what conditions
   - Discussion of potential confounding between phase variation (γ_i) and amplitude variation (v_i), and whether these sources of variation can be distinguished in the limit
   - Justification for why the larger equivalence classes under norm-preserving action (point iii, Section 2) are sufficient for reliable recovery beyond intuitive appeal

2. **Computational inefficiency severely limiting practical applicability**: The MCMC algorithm requires approximately 93-111 minutes for n=30 functions at T=50 discretization points (approximately 300,000 iterations). The authors explicitly state "We do not make any claims on efficiency of the computational algorithms" (Section 5). This computational burden is prohibitive for realistic datasets, which typically have n > 100 and T > 100. Scalability to larger datasets remains unexplored, and the paper provides:
   - No computational complexity analysis
   - No concrete proposals for algorithmic improvements (e.g., variational inference, expectation-propagation, gradient-based methods, parallel tempering)
   - No investigation of how computation scales with n and T
   - Limited discussion of practical solutions beyond acknowledging that "there is room for improvement"

3. **Hyperparameter specification and basis selection challenges**: While the authors argue that the norm-preserving action provides flexibility, practical guidance for hyperparameter selection is limited:
   - The number of basis functions B_f and B_r must be specified a priori, yet the paper provides no principled data-driven selection procedure
   - Appendix H shows that severe underspecification of B_f clearly degrades performance (Figure 10), contradicting claims that basis choice is "not crucial"
   - The choice between modified Fourier and B-splines for μ appears somewhat ad-hoc; Table 1 shows non-negligible differences between bases (e.g., Model 2-F vs Model 2-B), suggesting basis selection does impact results
   - Sensitivity to prior hyperparameters (e.g., θ_γ = 30 in PM2) is not thoroughly explored; while Appendix H shows robustness to over/under-specification, this analysis is limited to simulated data matching the model assumptions
   - Limited guidance for practitioners on how to choose B_f and B_r for new applications

4. **Restrictive modeling assumptions with limited robustness investigation**: The model assumes:
   - Common discretization across all observations
   - Independent Gaussian errors
   - Specific forms for basis functions
   - Independence of random effect coefficients
   
   While the authors note these can be relaxed "at some computational cost," the practical implications of violations are not thoroughly explored. Example 2 partially addresses robustness when data are generated from the value-preserving model, but more systematic investigation would strengthen claims. The paper does not adequately address:
   - Robustness to non-Gaussian errors or autocorrelated measurement error (common in functional data)
   - Performance with sparse or irregularly sampled data
   - Behavior when the true data-generating process deviates from model assumptions in other ways

## Minor Weaknesses and Concerns

1. **Limited scope of comparison**: Comparisons are primarily against warpMix (frequentist) and one Bayesian baseline (Cheng et al., 2016, relegated to Appendix A). More extensive comparisons with other functional mixed model approaches (e.g., those by Morris & Carroll, Guo, Chen & Wang) and modern machine learning methods would contextualize performance gains. Additionally, in Example 2, data are deliberately generated from warpMix's model, which may introduce favorable bias toward the proposed method.

2. **Unclear practical advantages in some cases**: While the proposed method sometimes outperforms warpMix, the improvements are not consistently large. In Table 1, Model 2-B achieves only 8.2% improvement over warpMix for μ_1. The paper does not provide clear guidance on when practitioners should expect meaningful improvements or what constitutes a practically significant difference.

3. **Phase function expressiveness trade-off**: PM1 restricts phase functions to γ(t) = t + α_i t(1-t), excluding inflection points and limiting expressiveness. While PM2 addresses this limitation through a nonparametric Dirichlet process-based prior, the computational cost increases substantially. The paper does not thoroughly explore:
   - How restrictive PM1 is in practice
   - The computational scalability of PM2
   - Guidance on when to use PM1 versus PM2

4. **Unclear practical interpretation of size-and-shape**: While mathematically well-defined, the practical interpretation of size-and-shape versus classical shape remains somewhat abstract. The paper does not clearly articulate:
   - Why practitioners analyzing Berkeley growth curves or ECG data should prefer recovering size-and-shape over shape
   - What meaningful insights size-and-shape provides in application domains
   - How to validate that recovered size-and-shape has meaningful interpretation when ground truth is unavailable (as in real data examples)

5. **Orbit centering procedure under-explained**:

Post-rebuttal: The rebuttal addressed most of my concerns and I would like to raise my score.

Strengths:
1）	This paper tries to solve a challenging task, recovering the size-and-shape of an unknown function by sampling. 
2）	The theoretical analysis is interesting, e.g., Derivation of Metropolis–Hastings acceptance ratio.
3）	The authors also give the convergence analysis by numerical experiments.

Weaknesses:
1)	It seems that we should give different prior models for different functions.
2)	The parameter sensitivity of $\theta$ and the running time of the proposed method should be given.

Questions:
1)	Neural networks can also recover functions by sampling. What are the advantages of the proposed method compared to neural networks?
2)	How can we recover arbitrary by a fixed model in Eq.(3)?

Rating:
5

Summary:
In this paper is proposed a mixed model in a functional Hilbert space for a size-and-shape (a type of geometric property) of a square-integrable fixed effect. To this end, the authors consider an isometric action of the infinite-dimensional group of phase functions. Synthetic and real experiments and comparisons with respect to another method in literature are reported, demonstrating how a posterior mean, and under the proposed model, captures the main properties of the function.

Strengths:
+ A Bayesian functional mixed effects model with an unrestricted form of phase variation is presented. 
 
+ A novel perspective of inferring a size-and-shape function, a type of geometric constraint. 
 
+ In general, the paper is concise and direct. Most explanations and discussions are provided. It is easy to follow, up to some points in formulation.

Weaknesses:
- I think the notation could be improved, representing, for example, the use of vectors, matrices and scalars differently. In some cases, including dimensionality may help readers. Moreover, some symbols are different along text (see the transpose operator). 
 
- A couple of linear combinations of basis functions are assumed. The rank of every subspace is Bf and Br. This type of formulation has been proposed in previous approaches, following an LDA style. The rank of every subspace is known a prior. 
 
- The authors could include a practical motivation of the study, where they could apply their approach and why it is important.

Questions:
How is the rank of both subspaces fixed? By hand? How sensitive is the solution with respect to these parameters? Could Bf and Br be inferred automatically? This seems to be a key factor in the model, but the authors do not provide any discussion in this line. 
 
The authors propose to use a B-spline basis, so a pre-defined one. Why this type of function? Note that we can find many piecewise polynomials in literature. 
 
Could both basis functions be learned jointly with weight coefficients? 
 
In real data, n=93, but when the authors use synthetic one, n=100,000. I cannot understand why the difference needs to be so big. In my opinion, if the goal is to sort out the problem in scenarios where the amount of data is limited, this should be also considered in the synthetic scenario. 
 
Figure 1 (b) and (c) are not cited in the introduction. 
 
The method consistently outperforms warpMix on synthetic data. While I like this comparison, I think the authors should consider more datasets for this evaluation. In addition to that, what about computational cost? I would like to see the trade-off accuracy vs. cost for both methods. 

What about failure cases?

Limitations:
The authors do not analyze properly the limitations of the paper. In any case, I do not think the work in this paper can produce a negative social impact.

Rating:
5
reviews.text:
# Hybrid Peer Review

## Summary

This paper proposes a Bayesian functional mixed effects model for inferring the size-and-shape of a fixed effect function µ in functional data exhibiting phase and amplitude variability. The key innovation is employing the norm-preserving action of phase functions (diffeomorphisms characterized by unitary transformations on the Hilbert space L²) rather than the traditional value-preserving action. This geometric perspective allows the authors to decompose observations as f_i = D_{γ_i}(µ + v_i + ε_i), where D_{γ_i} represents a size-and-shape-preserving rotation, v_i captures amplitude deviations, and ε_i is measurement error. The authors develop a complete Bayesian framework with two flexible prior models for phase functions (PM1: a one-parameter family; PM2: a nonparametric Dirichlet-process-based model), implement MCMC inference with detailed algorithmic specifications, and demonstrate superior performance compared to the state-of-the-art frequentist method warpMix on simulated and real datasets including Berkeley growth curves and ECG complexes.

## Strengths

+ **Novel theoretical perspective**: The use of norm-preserving actions to define and recover size-and-shape as a geometric property is creative and well-motivated. The distinction between value-preserving and norm-preserving actions is clearly articulated, and the connection to statistical shape analysis of landmark data provides excellent intuition for understanding why this approach may offer advantages.

+ **Mathematically rigorous framework**: The paper develops the functional mixed model with appropriate rigor, leveraging group theory and differential geometry. The treatment of the Lie group Γ of phase functions and its action on L² through unitary transformations is elegant and provides solid theoretical grounding. Detailed derivations in appendices support reproducibility.

+ **Comprehensive Bayesian framework**: Two flexible prior models for phase functions are proposed, with complete specifications for priors, likelihoods, and MCMC algorithms. The use of group structure in proposal distributions is clever, and the framework accommodates unrestricted nonparametric phase functions without restrictive rank assumptions on error covariance, addressing real limitations of prior work.

+ **Extensive experimental validation**: The paper includes extensive simulations, real data applications on multiple datasets, sensitivity analyses (Appendix H), and systematic comparisons with state-of-the-art methods. The empirical validation is thorough, with MCMC diagnostics (Appendix F) showing good convergence properties.

+ **Reproducibility**: Code, data, and detailed hyperparameter specifications are provided with clear instructions, facilitating independent verification of results.

+ **Honest acknowledgment of limitations**: The authors transparently discuss computational costs, acknowledge the lack of theoretical guarantees, and note modeling assumptions, which aids readers in understanding the scope of applicability.

## Major Weaknesses

- **Lack of theoretical justification and identifiability analysis**: The paper explicitly acknowledges (Section 5) that "what is lacking is theoretical support for the same, and this is work in progress." This is a significant gap for a methodological contribution. No identifiability results, convergence guarantees, or formal conditions are provided for when and why size-and-shape can be reliably recovered. The central claim that norm-preserving actions enable reliable recovery (point iii, Section 2, citing Bogachev 1998) is stated without rigorous proof in the functional data context. Specifically: (i) What prevents confounding between phase variation (γ_i) and amplitude variation (v_i) in the limit? (ii) Under what conditions on sample size n, discretization T, and prior specification is reliable recovery guaranteed? (iii) What is the posterior contraction rate? These questions remain unanswered, making it difficult to assess when practitioners should trust the method's inferences.

- **Severe computational inefficiency limiting practical applicability**: MCMC inference requires approximately 93-111 minutes for n=30 functions at T=50 discretization points (300,000 iterations). This computational burden is prohibitive for realistic datasets, which often have n>100 and T>100. The authors acknowledge this limitation but explicitly state (Section 5) "We do not make any claims on efficiency of the computational algorithms" and offer no concrete solutions. The paper does not explore whether scalability could be improved through variational inference, expectation-propagation, approximations, or parallelization strategies. This severely constrains the method's practical utility.

- **Hyperparameter specification without principled guidance**: The number of basis functions B_f and B_r must be specified a priori, and while Appendix H demonstrates robustness to over-specification, severe under-specification of B_f clearly degrades performance (Figure 10). The paper provides limited guidance for practitioners on how to choose these critical hyperparameters in new applications. A data-driven approach (e.g., cross-validation, information criteria) would strengthen the methodology. Similarly, the precision parameter θ_γ = 30 in PM2 is fixed without discussion of sensitivity to this choice.

- **Limited comparison scope and fairness concerns**: The primary comparison is against warpMix (frequentist); comparison to the Bayesian method (Cheng et al., 2016) is relegated to Appendix A. More problematically, Example 2 deliberately uses data generated from the warpMix model (value-preserving action) but fits the proposed norm-preserving model, which may introduce favorable bias and somewhat undermines the claim that norm-preserving action is necessary for reliable recovery. Comparisons to other functional mixed model approaches, registration methods, or modern alternatives would strengthen claims of superiority. Additionally, in the PQRST example, warpMix failed to produce estimates—it is unclear whether this represents a fundamental limitation or an implementation issue that could be resolved with different hyperparameters.

## Minor Weaknesses

- **Notation and presentation could be improved**: The notation could benefit from clearer distinction between vectors, matrices, and scalars, with explicit dimensionality indicators in key equations. The transpose operator, for instance, is represented inconsistently throughout the text. The connection between the continuous model (Equation 3) and its discretized implementation (Equations 4-6) deserves more intuitive explanation. Heavy use of operator notation (D_γ, composition, group operations) may limit accessibility to practitioners unfamiliar with functional data analysis and group theory. The distinction between norm-preserving and value-preserving actions in Figure 1(c) is subtle and deserves more detailed exposition in the main text.

- **Basis selection appears somewhat arbitrary**: While the authors argue that the norm-preserving action provides flexibility through rotation via D_γ, the practical impact of basis selection on performance is not thoroughly investigated. The choice of modified Fourier basis for µ and B-splines for v_i appears ad-hoc. The claim that basis choice is "not crucial" (Section 5) contradicts observed sensitivity in Table 1 (Model 1-F vs. Model 1-B show non-negligible differences). Appendix G shows poor performance with FPCA basis, suggesting the claim of flexibility may be overstated. Principled guidance on basis selection for new applications is lacking.

- **Restrictive phase variation in PM1**: The one-parameter family restricts phase functions to γ(t) = t + α_i t(1-t), excluding inflection points and limiting expressiveness. While PM2 addresses this through nonparametric modeling, this comes at substantial computational cost, and the practical trade-offs are not thoroughly explored.

- **Model assumptions constrain real-world applicability**: The model assumes common discretization across observations, independent Gaussian errors, and independence of random effect coefficients. While the authors note these can be relaxed "at some computational cost," the practical implications of violations are not explored empirically. How sensitive is the method to non-Gaussian errors, autocorrelated measurement error, or irregular discretization? Example 2 provides one robustness check, but more systematic investigation would strengthen claims.

- **Limited practical guidance on interpretation and applicability**: While the Berkeley and PQRST examples are motivating, the paper does not clearly articulate when practitioners should prefer size-and-shape recovery over classical shape recovery. The practical significance of this distinction for real applications remains somewhat abstract. Additionally, for real data examples where ground truth is unavailable, validation that recovered size-and-shape has meaningful interpretation in the application domain is lacking. Domain-expert assessments of the recovered µ would strengthen claims of practical utility.

- **Missing discussion of failure modes**: The paper does not systematically explore or discuss cases where the method might perform poorly. What happens when the true model substantially violates the norm-preserving action assumption? How does performance degrade with sparse or irregularly sampled data (mentioned as future work but not addressed)?

## Questions and Suggestions

1. **Theoretical foundations**: Can you provide any theoretical justification—even partial results on identifiability or consistency—for when size-and-shape can be reliably recovered? What formal conditions on n, T, and prior specification ensure reliable inference? Can you provide a rigorous proof

title: Chain of Preference Optimization: Improving Chain-of-Thought Reasoning in LLMs

reviews.summary: The paper introduces Chain-of-Preference Optimization (CPO) to improve reasoning in LLMs. CPO utilizes preference data generated during ToT to fine-tune models. This approach improves reasoning without increasing inference complexity.
reviews.strengths:
1. The idea of CPO is interesting.
2. CPO achieves enhanced performance without increasing inference complexity.
3. The paper is well-written and easy to follow.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. While CPO is effective across several tasks, its performance on more complex reasoning benchmarks, such as the MATH benchmark, remains unexplored.
2. Fine-tuning with reasoning paths generated by ToT may reduce the diversity of generated paths. A systematic investigation into how the generated reasoning paths change before and after fine-tuning is needed.
3. The effectiveness of CPO is heavily dependent on the quality of reasoning paths generated by ToT. If ToT produces suboptimal paths, the preference data might not be as beneficial.
4. The paper does not adequately address the applicability of CPO to more advanced models, such as LLaMA3.
5. While CPO enhances performance, the paper lacks a deep analysis of the interpretability of the optimized reasoning paths. Understanding why certain paths are preferred could provide additional insights and improve trust in the model’s decisions.
reviews.limitations: Yes
